title: [HotColdSplit] Respect "nooutline" attribute in HotColdSplit pass
author: SharonXSharon
description:
Swift uses atomic once-tokens for thread-safe lazy initialization of static variables. The once-wrapper functions (_WZ suffix) implement this pattern:
```
  if (once_token == initialized) return cached_value;  // hot path
  swift_once(&token, init);                            // cold path
  return cached_value;
```
Before Hot/Cold Splitting (correct):
```
  define double @"$s...value_WZ"() {
  entry:
    %token = load atomic i64, ptr @once_token seq_cst
    %initialized = icmp eq i64 %token, -1
    br i1 %initialized, label %already_init, label %need_init

  need_init:                                    ; cold path - executed once
    call void @swift_once(ptr @once_token, ptr @initializer)
    ; ... initialization logic ...
    br label %already_init

  already_init:
    %cached = load double, ptr @cached_value
    ret double %cached
  }
```
After Hot/Cold Splitting (broken):
```
  define double @"$s...value_WZ"() {
  entry:
    %token = load atomic i64, ptr @once_token seq_cst
    %initialized = icmp eq i64 %token, -1
    br i1 %initialized, label %already_init, label %codeRepl

  codeRepl:
    call void @"$s...value_WZ.cold.1"()   ; <-- cold path extracted
    br label %already_init

  already_init:
    %cached = load double, ptr @cached_value
    ret double %cached
  }

  define internal void @"$s...value_WZ.cold.1"() cold {
    call void @swift_once(ptr @once_token, ptr @initializer)
    ; ... initialization logic ...
    ret void
  }
```

The Problem:
Hot/Cold Splitting identifies `need_init` as cold (executed once during program lifetime) and extracts it into a separate `.cold function`. This breaks semantics because:

- The extracted cold function becomes a separate call, and the relationship between the token check and the swift_once call is no longer atomic within a single function's control flow.
- Interaction with other optimizations: When combined with function merging or other IPO passes, the outlined cold function may be merged with other  similar cold functions, further corrupting the control flow.
- Register/stack state: The cold path expects certain register or stack state that may not be properly preserved across the outlined call boundary.

Respect "nooutline" attribute to prevent hot-cold splitting of functions with that attribute. This allows frontends to mark functions that should not be split by function splitting passes like hot-cold splitting.

tests.test_body:
target datalayout = "e-m:o-i64:64-f80:128-n8:16:32:64-S128"
target triple = "arm64-apple-ios14.0"

define void @func_with_nooutline_attr(i1 %cond) #0 {
entry:
  br i1 %cond, label %fast_path, label %cold_path

fast_path:                                        ; preds = %entry
  ret void

cold_path:                                        ; preds = %entry
  call void @cold_helper()
  call void @cold_helper()
  call void @cold_helper()
  ret void
}

; Function Attrs: cold
declare void @cold_helper() #1

attributes #0 = { "nooutline" }
attributes #1 = { cold }

comments.author: kyulee-com
comments.body:
I'd like to understand more about the concrete breakage — `swift_once` provides the synchronization, so what exactly breaks when the cold path is extracted?
The name matching feels fragile. Would you consider emitting a function attribute from Swift's IRGen instead and checking it here?

comments.author: SharonXSharon
comments.body: > I'd like to understand more about the concrete breakage — `swift_once` provides the synchronization, so what exactly breaks when the cold path is extracted? So in the original code, Any thread that sees token == DONE is guaranteed to see all the initialization writes. While after HCS, the code became main function, cold.1 for the initialization, cold.2 for error out, cold.3 for reading the global. The issue is the memory barrier is only protecting the cold.1 function, so when 2 threads are trying to call this function simultaneously, even though thread 2 can't get the lock while thread1 is running the initialization in cold.1, there could be a race condition, where thread1 release the lock, thread2 get the lock, thread1 hasn't finished propagating the writing of the global, while thread2 already has tried to read the global, and got a garbage value.

comments.author: drodriguez
comments.body: Does hotcold split deals with globals? Because `Wz` should only be for globals, not functions, so it should not belong there (even if we go with a mangled name approach).

comments.author: SharonXSharon
comments.body:
> Does hotcold split deals with globals? Because `Wz` should only be for globals, not functions, so it should not belong there (even if we go with a mangled name approach).

It's _WZ suffix (both upper case)  , for the swift_once-wrapper functions IIUC

comments.author: nikic
comments.body:
> main function, cold.1 for the initialization, cold.2 for error out, cold.3 for reading the global. The issue is the memory barrier is only protecting the cold.1 function, so when 2 threads are trying to call this function simultaneously, even though thread 2 can't get the lock while thread1 is running the initialization in cold.1, there could be a race condition, where thread1 release the lock, thread2 get the lock, thread1 hasn't finished propagating the writing of the global, while thread2 already has tried to read the global, and got a garbage value.

I still don't really understand what the issue is. You say that "the memory barrier is only protecting the cold.1 function". Is this barrier in the sense of fence? A fence does not apply to a single function, it's a global operation.
